title: Carved oak mixing spoon
subtitle: A heavy green-oak spoon for pastry, bread, and batter work
excerpt: Oak makes a heavy, stiff mixing spoon with more resistance in the hand than ash or sycamore; well-suited to stiff bread doughs and pastry mixes. The spoon is deeper-bowled than a serving spoon and the handle is thick enough to take the torque of heavy mixing. Green oak carves cleanly before the tannins fully fix.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for oak treen and kitchen utensils; Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) references oak and sycamore for kitchen implements.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled or recently riven timber, still high in moisture content. Easier to carve before the tannins in oak fully fix and the fibres harden.
Split along the grain with a froe and maul, rather than sawn across it. A riven billet is stronger than a sawn equivalent and shows the grain clearly.
The rough-shaped piece of wood from which the finished item is carved.
The safe default cut: blade travelling away from the body, driven by the forearm, cutting off the end of the workpiece into open air.

content.text: Oak makes the heaviest and stiffest spoon in the treen canon. That weight is a liability for a salad server but an asset for a mixing spoon: the extra mass carries the blade through a stiff bread dough without the maker having to force it. This is a working implement rather than a display piece. The
blank
comes from
green

oak; carve it before the tannins set and the wood will cut with an axe and sloyd knife without undue effort. A caution on oak and iron tools: wipe the blank and the tools after carving sessions longer than 30 minutes. The tannins in oak react with iron to produce a blue-black stain. Wipe the blank with a damp cloth and the tools with a dry one; the stain is superficial but will appear in the finished piece if ignored.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood
A
riven

green oak billet: 32 cm long, 9 cm across the face, 5 cm deep. English and sessile oak both work; the grain on a freshly rived piece runs straight and true. Source from a tree surgeon or community woodland; oak splits readily with a froe from a log in the 30 to 40 cm diameter range. Sycamore or ash serve as alternatives if fresh oak is not available; they are lighter and will make a more slender mixing spoon.
Method
Draw the profile: handle 22 cm, neck 2 cm, bowl oval 7 cm long and 5.5 cm wide. Mark the bowl depth at 3 cm on the side face.
Rough the profile with the carving axe, leaving 6 mm to the pencil line. Use
push cuts
on the sloyd knife for the refinement pass.

Shape the handle to an oval cross-section, 22 mm across the widest face and 17 mm deep; thick enough for a firm grip during mixing. The handle axis should be in line with the bowl axis; a twisted mixing spoon is uncomfortable to use.

Hollow the bowl with the large hook knife. The bowl of a mixing spoon should be deeper than a serving spoon: 3 cm at the lowest point. Work from rim to centre with the grain, which in a mixing-spoon blank runs roughly along the handle axis. Cutting across the grain in the bowl will cause the hook knife to tear rather than shave.
Wipe the blank and the tools after each session. The tannin-iron reaction produces a blue-black mark that can be sanded out before finishing but is easier to prevent.

Leave to dry slowly; 3 weeks for green oak is the minimum. Oak dries slowly and evenly compared to ash. A hairline check on the end grain of the handle is common and is not a structural problem if the check does not run into the bowl area.
Finishing
Sand 120 and 180 grit; apply raw linseed oil; leave an hour; wipe off. Two-week cure before food contact. Oak takes a linseed finish with a golden tone that deepens with use.
Variations
Pastry spoon: the bowl angled at 10 to 15 degrees to the handle axis; useful for scraping the sides of a mixing bowl.
Left-handed: carve the neck angle for a left-handed mixing motion; the bowl should face inward on the non-dominant side.
Care
Wash with warm water; towel-dry immediately. The oak tannins will continue to react with iron if left wet in a metal sink. Re-oil monthly.

items.symptom: Blue-black stain appears on the blank surface
items.cause: Oak tannins reacting with the iron in cutting tools
items.fix: Wipe the blank with a damp cloth and the tools with a dry cloth after every session. Sand the stain out before finishing; raw linseed oil does not cover it.

items.symptom: Grain tears in long ragged chips during axe work
items.cause: Cutting against the grain direction in oak; more pronounced than in sycamore
items.fix: Read the grain from the side face before each axe stroke. Oak grain follows the ray lines visible on the riven face; cutting against them produces long splits rather than chips.
